citations.quote: Generalised epilepsy with documented generalised spike-and-wave activity on prior EEG (2017)
Sodium valproate 500 mg bd
Clobazam 10 mg nocte (as rescue on cluster days)
Over the last four weeks, the records note: "Cluster days twice this month; typically six seizures in 24 h."
These events are brief generalised convulsions with rapid recovery, no new focal features, and no injuries reported.
He describes a prodrome of diffuse head pressure and photophobia on days preceding clusters, occasionally with poor sleep the night before.
There is no clear relation to intercurrent illness, but stress and missed meals were mentioned as possible triggers.

Where rescue has been needed, clobazam 10 mg nocte has been used for up to three consecutive nights with good effect and without excessive daytime sedation.

citations.quote: Patient has generalised epilepsy.
Sodium valproate 500 mg twice daily
Clobazam 10 mg at night as needed for jet-lag periods (patient-reported intermittent use)
Two events over the last five months.
His first seizure occurred in May 2019 in Ireland, at night while asleep.
He woke with rhythmic twitching of the right arm and a sense of déjà vu.
The second event was in October 2019 in Scotland, also during sleep, lasting five minutes with a similar pattern of symptoms.
he is a frequent business traveller with irregular sleep and time zones.
He reports adherence to valproate on most days but occasionally misses doses during long-haul flights.
No alcohol excess reported; significant sleep disruption around both events.
Arrange routine EEG at next in-person visit when in London; if logistics difficult, consider ambulatory EEG coordinated around his travel.

citations.quote: Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset.
Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset.

Current concerns: She reports that over the past three months she has had brief clusters of events described as “jaw tightness then body goes stiff on the right, and I can’t get words out”, typically lasting under a minute with rapid recovery.
Medication: Lamotrigine modified-release 200 mg twice daily. Rescue: buccal midazolam 10 mg PRN prolonged events (not used in past year).
Investigations: Prior MRI (2016) reported no definite epileptogenic lesion. Routine EEG in 2019 showed intermittent left temporal slowing without epileptiform discharges. No recent bloods on file.

Current concerns: She reports that over the past three months she has had brief clusters of events described as “jaw tightness then body goes stiff on the right, and I can’t get words out”, typically lasting under a minute with rapid recovery.

Aura/ictal description (patient-reported): “A rising wave in my stomach, a metallic taste, and sounds feel far away for a few seconds. If I catch it early and sit down, it sometimes fades; if I’ve missed tablets, it goes into the jaw clamping and right arm stiffening.”
No clear generalisation reported. Postictal phase minimal; feels briefly foggy.
She links these to missed doses of anti-seizure medication and has kept a diary demonstrating a temporal association.
Sleep fragmented due to work shifts.
She has set phone alarms but reports these are sometimes ignored during busy shifts.

Safety: reiterated individual seizure triggers (sleep loss, missed doses) and advised continued use of aura recognition strategies, sitting/lying down when warnings occur, and maintaining diary entries.
Overall seizure pattern is best characterised as focal tonic with missed ASM doses.
Diagnoses/summary: Focal epilepsy, likely temporal lobe onset.

citations.quote: Recurrent seizures of uncertain classification with increasing frequency and situational sensitivity.
Levetiracetam 500 mg twice daily
Clonazepam 0.5 mg nocte as rescue for clusters (patient-reported, used sparingly)
As of this month she reports 2 seizure events; two in August, 7 in July and 2 in June during both sleep and wakefulness.

Events are described as brief episodes of behavioural arrest with loss of awareness, sometimes preceded by a rising internal sensation and flashing lights in her vision, occasionally progressing to bilateral stiffening and jerking lasting under two minutes, with post-event confusion and headache.
There is no tongue biting or urinary incontinence reported in the majority of episodes.
Her partner has witnessed two daytime collapses at work, both temporally associated with exposure to overhead flicker.
She has started a new role in an open-plan office where strong and intermittently flickering lighting is present, and she has noticed more events in that environment.
Sleep deprivation and stress appear to exacerbate frequency.
Arrange urgent EEG and MRI brain with epilepsy protocol
Bloods today: U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D; Levetiracetam level if clinically indicated

citations.quote: Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness
Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness

Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily; Carbamazepine (Tegretol Retard) 400 mg twice daily; Topiramate 75 mg morning and 100 mg evening; Atorvastatin 20 mg once daily; Lansoprazole 30 mg once daily; Bisoprolol 5 mg once daily; Ramipril 5 mg once daily; Thiamine 100 mg once daily; Vitamin B compound strong one tablet daily; Calcium/Vitamin D supplements; Carbocisteine 750 mg twice daily; Emollients as required.
stereotyped focal impaired-awareness episodes featuring a rising epigastric sensation, brief arrest of speech, right facial pulling, and post-event confusion for several minutes.
he and staff document 5 or 7 focal onset seizures in three weeks during a recent period
Outside that three-week window, there have been no further episodes for the last six weeks.
He links this cluster to marked anticipatory and in-flight anxiety, sleep disruption the night before travel, and missed hydration.
one evening dose of levetiracetam may have been late on the day of outbound travel.
Bloods: request U&E, LFT, FBC, vitamin D, and carbamazepine level via practice in the next two weeks.
Bone health: continue calcium/vitamin D; DEXA recheck due next year.
focal onset seizures
There has been no secondary generalisation, tongue biting, or injuries.

citations.quote: the episodes are now occurring abs monthly, with intervening weeks free of events.
His medications include ramipril for hypertension and vitamin D.
He is not on any antiseizure medication currently.
Previous investigations: routine EEG one year ago reportedly normal; MRI brain without contrast five years ago for headaches was described as normal.
I have not yet reviewed the original images or tracings.
I have discussed prolonged EEG monitoring with home ambulatory recording as a first step, followed by inpatient video-EEG if required.

I will also arrange an MRI brain with epilepsy protocol to ensure high-resolution temporal lobe coverage, and I have requested thyroid function and metabolic profile via his GP to exclude contributory systemic factors.
paroxysmal episodes with impaired awareness and post-event confusion.
a brief prodrome of tightness across the chest, a rising internal "surge", and an abrupt loss of awareness witnessed by his partner.
On most occasions there is bilateral hand fumbling and a brief vocalisation.
He does not recall the episodes and is usually confused for 10–15 minutes afterwards, occasionally with a dull bifrontal headache.
He denies tongue biting or incontinence.
There is no clear motor convulsion described.
He links recent clustering to periods of poor sleep and heavy workload.
on days with very high caffeine use and reduced sleep, he feels more jittery and has experienced the prodromal "surge" without progression on two occasions.
Sleep is fragmented during busy periods (often <6 hours).
The semiology is not sufficiently specific to assign a definitive classification at this stage.
